summary: A collection of interviews, speeches, and reflections by Steve Jobs spanning his childhood, the founding of Apple, the creation of the Macintosh, and his 1985 departure from the company. The excerpts highlight his philosophies on intuitive product design, the intersection of technology and the liberal arts, and his deep-seated drive to contribute to human progress by building wonderful things.

principles.statement: Express your appreciation to humanity by making something wonderful.
principles.rationale: Even if you never meet the people who use your product, the care and love you put into making it transmits your deep appreciation to the rest of the species.
principles.source_quote: But one of the ways that I believe people express their appreciation to the rest of humanity is to make something wonderful and put it out there.

principles.statement: The complex things around us are just human creations that anyone can understand and build.
principles.rationale: Demystifying technology gives people the self-confidence to explore, learn, and build things themselves rather than viewing the world as full of magical, incomprehensible objects.
principles.source_quote: Things became much more clear that they were the results of human creation, not these magical things that just appeared in one’s environment that one had no knowledge of their interiors.

principles.statement: Making a product truly great requires time and perseverance, not necessarily more money.
principles.rationale: It doesn't take more energy or money to refine a product to greatness, just the stubbornness and willingness to keep going until it is as good as it can be.
principles.source_quote: if you’re going to make something, it doesn’t take any more energy— and rarely does it take more money— to make it really great. All it takes is a little more time. Not that much more. And a willingness to do so, a willingness to persevere until it’s really great.

principles.statement: We have a responsibility to put something back into the pool of human experience.
principles.rationale: We constantly take from society by using clothes, food, language, and math developed by others. We should use our time and technology to contribute back to that shared pool.
principles.source_quote: And the ability to put something back into the pool of human experience is extremely neat.

mental_models.name: The Telegraph vs. The Telephone
mental_models.description: A historical analogy for evaluating user interfaces and technology adoption. The telegraph was a breakthrough but required learning complex codes, limiting its use. The telephone was intuitive and allowed for nuance and expression.
mental_models.example: Jobs used this to explain why the IBM PC (which required learning 'slash-qz's' like Morse code) wouldn't improve productivity for everyone, whereas the Macintosh (the 'telephone' of the industry) let users 'sing' with fonts and graphics.

mental_models.name: The Magician Analogy
mental_models.description: A metaphor to explain how computers work to non-technical people. Computers execute incredibly mundane, simple instructions, but they do them at such high speeds that the result appears magical.
mental_models.example: Jobs compared a computer's processing to a person running out to grab flowers and returning in the blink of an eye. The steps are simple, but the speed creates the illusion of magic.

heuristics: Your aesthetics get better as you make mistakes.
Whenever you start with nothing, you can always shoot for the moon. You have nothing to lose.
You work for Apple first and your boss second.

signature_quotes.text: But one of the ways that I believe people express their appreciation to the rest of humanity is to make something wonderful and put it out there.
signature_quotes.context: Reflecting on how people can express their deep appreciation for life and humanity.

signature_quotes.text: We’re trying to get away from programming. We’ve got to get away from programming because people don’t want to program computers. People want to use computers.
signature_quotes.context: Speaking at the International Design Conference in Aspen about the future of personal computing.

signature_quotes.text: What I’m best at doing is finding a group of talented people and making things with them.
signature_quotes.context: Discussing his future plans after being ousted from Apple in 1985.

signature_quotes.text: Your aesthetics get better as you make mistakes.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining his approach to product design and refinement.

anti_patterns.description: Slipping into 'cover-your-ass mode' after achieving success.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It makes you conservative and prevents you from shooting for the moon and innovating the way you did when you had nothing to lose.

anti_patterns.description: Forcing users to learn programming or complex commands to use a computer.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Most people will not learn special incantations (like 'slash-qz's' or Morse code). People want to use computers as intuitive tools, not program them.

anti_patterns.description: Treating industrial design as an afterthought for computers.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: People will spend hours a day interacting with these machines, more than they do with cars. Making them look like 'garbage' wastes the opportunity to put a beautiful, communicative object in their environment.
